claim: Bun consolidates 10+ JavaScript tools (Node, npm/yarn/pnpm, TypeScript, Jest, ts-node, Vite, bundlers, dotenv) into single unified command - dramatically reduces context switching and setup complexity
actionable: For new JavaScript projects, use `bun init` and `bun run`/`bun install`/`bun test` to eliminate tool fragmentation and reduce development friction

claim: Bun doesn't require TypeScript transpiler setup - transparently executes .ts, .tsx, .js, .jsx files without intermediate build step, reducing toolchain complexity
actionable: Write TypeScript directly and run with `bun run file.ts` without configuring ts-node or TypeScript compilation

claim: Bun natively reads environment variables from .env files without dotenv library import - reduces boilerplate and dependencies
actionable: Remove dotenv imports and just use process.env - Bun automatically loads .env files without explicit configuration

claim: Bun mixes CommonJS and ES6 modules seamlessly in same file - abstracts away module compatibility issues that plagued JavaScript for 5+ years
actionable: Use require() and import in same codebase without workarounds - Bun handles interop automatically

claim: Bun built-in file I/O APIs (Bun.file, Bun.write) eliminate fs module - one cohesive API for filesystem operations vs Node's callback/Promise inconsistencies
actionable: Use Bun.write() and Bun.file() instead of fs module for cleaner, more consistent file operations

claim: Bun's main risk is VC funding model - owned by Oven (private company) creates lock-in risk if ecosystem adopts then monetizes later, despite current MIT/free positioning
actionable: Use Bun but maintain awareness that VC incentives could lead to pricing/lock-in changes 5+ years out; avoid total ecosystem migration until maturity

claim: Bun development is new/unstable - enterprise tools with poor documentation (Workday, Rippling) require manual integration because agent dev environments don't exist, limiting automation potential
actionable: For greenfield projects use Bun heavily; for existing/enterprise tool integration, plan for manual work and thorough testing before relying on Bun automation

claim: Bun CLI argument parsing is trivial - access via Bun.argv, enabling quick CLI tool creation without external parsing libraries
actionable: Build CLI tools in Bun without argparse/yargs by directly accessing command-line args
